text: The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the mesenteric root in a medial to lateral direction at the ligament of Treitz, using the uncinate first approach while positioned on the patient's left side.
The surgeon exposes the pancreatic head and major vessels early in the operation, allowing for safe mobilization of the ascending mesocolon.
Following the dorsal dissection, the surgeon incises the ascending mesocolon through a peritoneal window, carefully preserving the iliacolic vessels visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon continues the incision across the mesenteric root at the base of the transverse mesocolon, further mobilizing the mesocolon.
The surgeon dissects the superior mesenteric vein and performs lymphadenectomy up to the base of the mesocolon, carefully removing lymphatic tissue surrounding the vein.
The Heimler trunk veins are exposed, becoming visible in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows the pancreatic head and major vessels exposed, but no large vessels are divided at this stage of the operation.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac by incising along the greater curvature of the stomach to excise the gastro-mental package.
The surgeon divides erythrogastric adhesions connecting the mesocolon to the pancreas, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum along the first part of the duodenum, revealing underlying structures and facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the gastromental artery and progressively clips it, ensuring precise control over the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to identify the gastromental vein but is unable to locate it, which may result in bleeding during subsequent stages of the procedure.
Following the division of the right gastromental artery, the surgeon mobilizes the mesogastrium from the pancreatic head, dissecting along the inferior border of the pancreas.
The middle colic vein and the superior right colic vein, which runs into the healer trunk, are visible in the surgical field.

Bleeding from the right gastro-mental vein is observed. The surgeon manages the bleeding by increasing CO₂ inflation pressure to twenty millimeters of mercury and applying bipolar coagulation to the affected area.
The surgeon continues the operation inferior to the mesocolon, focusing on the anatomical structures and tissues in this region.
The surgeon defines the plane in the lesser sac and identifies the lower border of the pancreas, allowing for the safe division of the mesocolic vessels.
The surgeon encircles and clips the middle colic artery at the level of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), securing the vessel with surgical clips.
The surgeon divides the pancreatic or duodenal vein, followed by the division of the hamlet trunk at its confluence with the superior mesenteric vein, using surgical instruments.
The surgical field reveals the absence of a classical middle colic vein running into the superior mesenteric vein, indicating anatomical variation.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic vein joining the splenic vein, deviating from the typical anatomical pathway where it would connect to the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon divides the lateral adhesions on the right side of the patient, completing the final step of the operation.
The surgical field displays complete lymphatic clearance at the inferior border of the pancreas and around the superior mesenteric vessels, with visible absence of lymphatic tissue in these areas.
The surgeon, now positioned on the right side of the patient, performs a medial to lateral mobilization of the splenic flexure, beginning posterior to the mesenteric vein.
The surgeon divides the vein and dissects the transverse mesocolon away from the tail of the pancreas, separating these structures with precise surgical technique.
The surgeon removes the gastromental vascular arcade from the stomach, extending the dissection cranially up to the level of the spleen.
The surgeon mobilizes the entire left colon laterally, carefully following the anatomical plane of Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon reaches the medial dissection plane inferior to the splenic flexure, continuing the mobilization of the colon in this region.
The surgeon carefully dissects and selectively divides the left gastromental vessels, a challenging step in the procedure, with precise manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon approaches the dissection from the medial aspect, focusing on separating the transverse mesocolon from the tail of the pancreas.
The surgeon carefully dissects the splenic hilum, ensuring precise separation of tissues in this delicate area.
The surgeon isolates and divides a small artery, likely part of the left gastromental vessels, within the dissection area near the splenic hilum.
The surgeon uses an energy device to securely close and divide a larger vein, completing this step of the procedure.
The surgeon completes the division of additional adhesions at the splenic hilum, resulting in the transverse colon being fully mobilized and free from surrounding tissue.
The surgical view displays a clip placed on the left gastro-mental vein at its junction with the splenic vein.
The surgeon isolates and divides the left colic vessels, completing the dissection of the remaining vascular structures in the splenic hilum.
The surgeon dissects the left colic vessels close to their origin at the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon manages a small bleeding site by increasing the CO₂ inflation pressure and applying a clip to control the bleeding.
Following the division of the left colic artery, the surgeon mobilizes the left colon, progressing down to the rectosigmoid junction, with visible separation of the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgical field displays complete clearance of mesocolic and gastro-mental tissue, with the pancreas intact and the stomach visibly well-perfused.
